title: The Translator and the Author She Never Met
summary: An interview with a literary translator who spent eleven years rendering a Portuguese novelist into English and corresponded with him weekly, only for them to decide, deliberately, never to meet — an exploration of intimacy across language that depends on distance.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: You translated four of Almeida's novels, exchanged letters with him for over a decade, and yet you never once met in person. He lived in Porto; you live in Edinburgh. That's a ninety-minute flight. People find that almost perverse. Why not meet?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: I understand why it seems perverse. But it wasn't an accident, and it wasn't shyness. We discussed it, more than once, and we agreed. I think we both sensed that meeting would damage something we'd built precisely on the condition of not meeting. There's a kind of intimacy that only survives at a certain distance. Light a candle and bring it too close to glass and the glass cracks.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That sounds like a justification for avoidance, though. Forgive me, but couldn't you have had both — the letters and the friendship?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: You may be right that I'm rationalising. I've wondered. But consider what the work actually required. To translate a man, I had to inhabit his sentences from the inside — his rhythms, his evasions, the particular way he'd circle a grief for three pages before naming it. I knew his prose more intimately than his wife did. What I did not know, and chose not to know, was the sound of his voice, his table manners, whether he interrupted. Those things would have arrived and overwritten the man I'd assembled from his syntax. I'd have had to translate a stranger.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: But surely the real man is more authentic than the one you assembled from his sentences?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: Now that's the assumption I'd push back on. Why is the man at the dinner table the real one and the man in the prose a construction? He laboured over those sentences for years. They were not less him than his table manners — arguably more, because they were the version of himself he chose and revised. We meet most people only in their unrevised state. I had the rare privilege of knowing someone almost entirely through the self he'd most carefully made.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Did the letters ever stray into the personal? Or was it strictly about the work?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: Oh, deeply personal — but personal in writing, which is a different country. He told me things, I suspect, that he told no one he could see. There's a confessional freedom in writing to someone you'll never have to face across a room. He once wrote that I was the only person who had ever read him correctly, and that this was only possible because I would always be slightly imaginary to him. He needed me imaginary. And, if I'm honest, I needed him the same way.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Some would say that's not a real relationship — that you were each in love with a projection.

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: I'd half agree. We were partly in love with projections. But show me a relationship that isn't, at least partly, the same thing. The difference is only that ours never had to survive the daily collision with reality that erodes the projection in an ordinary friendship. Whether that makes it less real or more concentrated — purer, even, if that doesn't sound too precious — I genuinely don't know. I'm not claiming it's the better way to know a person. I'm claiming it's a way, and that something true passed between us that a meeting would have spent.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: He died last spring. Do you regret it now — never having met him?

body.speaker: Translator
body.text: Every day, and not at all. Both are true and I've stopped trying to resolve them. What I don't regret is the work. When I translate his last sentences — he was working on a fifth novel when he died, unfinished — I hear him with total clarity, and the clarity is undisturbed by any memory of a face. He stayed, for me, exactly the size of his sentences. There's a grief in that, but there's also a strange completeness. I lost a correspondent. I never had to lose a friend, because I'd been wise enough, or coward enough, never to fully acquire one. I'll let you decide which word is fairer. I've never managed to.

annotations.meaningVi: trái khoáy, ngược đời, cố tình làm khác lẽ thường
đó không phải vì nhút nhát

annotations.phrase: built precisely on the condition of not meeting
annotations.meaningVi: được xây dựng chính trên điều kiện không gặp mặt
một khoảng cách nhất định

annotations.phrase: Light a candle and bring it too close to glass
annotations.meaningVi: Thắp một ngọn nến và đưa nó quá gần tấm kính — ẩn dụ: gần quá thì rạn vỡ
một lời biện minh cho sự né tránh
tự bào chữa, hợp lý hóa một cách khiên cưỡng

annotations.phrase: inhabit his sentences from the inside
annotations.meaningVi: sống bên trong những câu văn của ông từ phía trong (thấu hiểu tận cùng)
những sự né tránh của ông (cách ông lảng tránh điều gì)
lượn vòng quanh một nỗi đau (nói tránh mãi mới gọi tên)

annotations.phrase: more intimately than his wife did
annotations.meaningVi: thân mật/sâu sắc hơn cả vợ ông
cách cư xử trên bàn ăn
ghi đè lên con người tôi đã lắp ghép (từ văn phong)
cú pháp, cách sắp xếp câu
phản bác, không đồng tình với
một sự dựng lên, một thứ được kiến tạo
đã dày công lao động trên (chăm chút từng chút)
ở trạng thái chưa chỉnh sửa (con người thô mộc, chưa gọt giũa)
lạc sang chuyện riêng tư
một đất nước khác — ẩn dụ: viết là một thế giới khác hẳn nói
sự tự do thú nhận/giãi bày
đối diện trực tiếp trong một căn phòng
đọc/hiểu ông một cách đúng đắn
hơi có phần tưởng tượng, không hoàn toàn thật
một sự phóng chiếu (hình ảnh ta tự gán cho người khác)
sự va chạm hằng ngày với thực tế
mài mòn dần sự phóng chiếu
cô đặc hơn — tinh khiết hơn
quá kiểu cách, màu mè (về cách diễn đạt)
một cuộc gặp hẳn đã tiêu xài hết (làm cạn kiệt điều quý giá đó)

annotations.phrase: undisturbed by any memory of a face
annotations.meaningVi: không bị quấy nhiễu bởi bất kỳ ký ức về một gương mặt nào

annotations.phrase: exactly the size of his sentences
annotations.meaningVi: đúng bằng kích cỡ của những câu văn ông — ông tồn tại trọn vẹn trong chữ nghĩa
đủ khôn ngoan, hay đủ hèn nhát — sự mơ hồ mà bà không thể tự phân định

grammarNotes.title: Concessive 'may'/'I'd half agree' and holding two truths at once
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người dịch dùng nhiều cấu trúc nhượng bộ một phần ('You may be right', 'I'd half agree', 'Both are true') để vừa thừa nhận lập luận của người phỏng vấn vừa giữ lập trường riêng. Đây là kỹ thuật C1 để thể hiện tư duy sắc bén, không phòng thủ, biết ôm chứa mâu thuẫn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Partial concession lets a speaker grant ground without surrendering: 'You may be right that I'm rationalising' and 'I'd half agree. We were partly in love with projections.' The clearest case is the paradox 'Every day, and not at all. Both are true and I've stopped trying to resolve them' — the structure refuses the interviewer's either/or and asserts that two opposed things can hold simultaneously.

translationVi:
Interviewer: Bà đã dịch bốn cuốn tiểu thuyết của Almeida, trao đổi thư từ với ông hơn một thập kỷ, vậy mà chưa một lần gặp mặt trực tiếp. Ông sống ở Porto; bà sống ở Edinburgh. Đó là một chuyến bay chín mươi phút. Người ta thấy điều đó gần như trái khoáy. Tại sao không gặp nhau?
Translator: Tôi hiểu vì sao nó có vẻ trái khoáy. Nhưng đó không phải là tình cờ, và cũng không phải vì nhút nhát. Chúng tôi đã bàn về nó, hơn một lần, và chúng tôi đồng ý. Tôi nghĩ cả hai chúng tôi đều cảm nhận được rằng gặp nhau sẽ làm hỏng một thứ mà chúng tôi đã xây dựng chính trên điều kiện không gặp mặt. Có một loại thân mật chỉ tồn tại được ở một khoảng cách nhất định. Thắp một ngọn nến và đưa nó quá gần tấm kính thì tấm kính nứt.
Interviewer: Nhưng nghe có vẻ như đó là một lời biện minh cho sự né tránh. Xin thứ lỗi, nhưng chẳng phải bà có thể có cả hai — thư từ và tình bạn — hay sao?
Translator: Có thể anh đúng rằng tôi đang tự bào chữa. Tôi đã từng băn khoăn. Nhưng hãy xét xem công việc thực sự đòi hỏi điều gì. Để dịch một con người, tôi phải sống bên trong những câu văn của ông từ phía trong — nhịp điệu của ông, những sự né tránh của ông, cái cách đặc thù ông lượn vòng quanh một nỗi đau suốt ba trang trước khi gọi tên nó. Tôi biết văn ông thân mật hơn cả vợ ông. Điều tôi không biết, và chọn không biết, là âm thanh giọng nói của ông, cách ông cư xử trên bàn ăn, liệu ông có hay ngắt lời không. Những thứ ấy hẳn sẽ ập đến và ghi đè lên con người tôi đã lắp ghép từ cú pháp của ông. Hẳn tôi sẽ phải dịch một người xa lạ.
Interviewer: Nhưng chắc chắn con người thật thì đích thực hơn con người bà lắp ghép từ những câu văn chứ?
Translator: Đó chính là giả định tôi muốn phản bác. Tại sao người ngồi ở bàn ăn lại là người thật còn người trong văn lại là một sự dựng lên? Ông đã dày công với những câu văn đó suốt nhiều năm. Chúng không phải là ông ít hơn cách cư xử trên bàn ăn của ông — có thể là nhiều hơn, vì chúng là phiên bản của chính ông mà ông đã chọn và chỉnh sửa. Chúng ta gặp hầu hết mọi người chỉ ở trạng thái chưa chỉnh sửa của họ. Tôi có cái đặc ân hiếm hoi được biết một người gần như hoàn toàn qua cái tôi mà ông đã chăm chút tạo dựng nhất.
Interviewer: Những lá thư có bao giờ lạc sang chuyện riêng tư không? Hay chỉ thuần về công việc?
Translator: Ồ, riêng tư sâu sắc — nhưng riêng tư trong chữ viết, vốn là một đất nước khác. Ông kể với tôi những điều, tôi ngờ rằng, ông không kể với ai mà ông có thể nhìn thấy. Có một sự tự do giãi bày khi viết cho người mà bạn sẽ không bao giờ phải đối diện trong một căn phòng. Có lần ông viết rằng tôi là người duy nhất từng đọc ông một cách đúng đắn, và rằng điều đó chỉ có thể vì tôi sẽ luôn hơi có phần tưởng tượng với ông. Ông cần tôi là tưởng tượng. Và, nếu thành thật, tôi cũng cần ông như vậy.
Interviewer: Một số người sẽ nói đó không phải là một mối quan hệ thật — rằng mỗi người đã yêu một sự phóng chiếu.
Translator: Tôi đồng ý một nửa. Chúng tôi phần nào đã yêu những sự phóng chiếu. Nhưng hãy chỉ cho tôi một mối quan hệ nào không, ít nhất một phần, là điều tương tự. Khác biệt duy nhất là mối quan hệ của chúng tôi chưa bao giờ phải sống sót qua sự va chạm hằng ngày với thực tế vốn mài mòn sự phóng chiếu trong một tình bạn thông thường. Liệu điều đó làm nó kém thật hay cô đặc hơn — tinh khiết hơn, thậm chí, nếu nghe không quá màu mè — tôi thực sự không biết. Tôi không khẳng định đó là cách tốt hơn để biết một con người. Tôi khẳng định đó là một cách, và rằng có điều gì đó chân thật đã đi qua giữa chúng tôi mà một cuộc gặp hẳn đã tiêu xài hết.
Interviewer: Ông mất mùa xuân năm ngoái. Bây giờ bà có hối tiếc không — vì đã không bao giờ gặp ông?
Translator: Mỗi ngày, và chẳng chút nào. Cả hai đều đúng và tôi đã thôi cố gắng hòa giải chúng. Điều tôi không hối tiếc là công việc. Khi tôi dịch những câu cuối của ông — ông đang viết dở cuốn tiểu thuyết thứ năm khi mất — tôi nghe thấy ông với sự rõ ràng tuyệt đối, và sự rõ ràng đó không bị quấy nhiễu bởi bất kỳ ký ức về một gương mặt nào. Với tôi, ông đã ở lại đúng bằng kích cỡ những câu văn của ông. Có một nỗi đau trong điều đó, nhưng cũng có một sự trọn vẹn kỳ lạ. Tôi mất một người bạn thư từ. Tôi chưa bao giờ phải mất một người bạn, vì tôi đã đủ khôn ngoan, hay đủ hèn nhát, để không bao giờ thực sự có được một người. Tôi sẽ để anh quyết định từ nào công bằng hơn. Tôi chưa bao giờ làm được điều đó.

questions.prompt: Why did the translator and Almeida never meet?
options: They could never agree on a convenient time
They deliberately agreed not to, sensing a meeting would damage what they had built on the condition of not meeting
Almeida was too ill to travel
The translator was afraid of flying
questions.explanation: 'We discussed it ... and we agreed ... meeting would damage something we'd built precisely on the condition of not meeting.'
questions.hint: The translator's first answer explains it was deliberate.

questions.prompt: What does the candle-and-glass image illustrate?
options: That their friendship was fragile and easily broken by argument
That a certain intimacy survives only at a distance and cracks if brought too close
That Almeida's prose was warm but dangerous
questions.explanation: 'There's a kind of intimacy that only survives at a certain distance. Light a candle and bring it too close to glass and the glass cracks.'
questions.hint: The translator uses the image right after mentioning distance.

questions.prompt: What did the translator say she knew about Almeida, and what did she choose NOT to know?
options: She knew his voice but not his prose
She knew his prose intimately but chose not to know his voice, table manners, or whether he interrupted
She knew his family but not his writing
She knew his opinions but not his syntax
questions.explanation: 'I knew his prose more intimately than his wife did. What I did not know, and chose not to know, was the sound of his voice, his table manners, whether he interrupted.'
questions.hint: The translator contrasts two kinds of knowledge in her third speech.

questions.prompt: When the interviewer says the 'real man' is more authentic than the one assembled from sentences, how does the translator respond?
options: She fully agrees and admits she missed the real man
She pushes back, arguing the prose was the self he chose and revised, perhaps more truly him than his table manners
She says authenticity does not matter to translators
She says she could not have known the real man anyway
questions.explanation: 'Why is the man at the dinner table the real one and the man in the prose a construction? ... They were not less him ... arguably more, because they were the version of himself he chose and revised.'
questions.hint: The translator challenges the interviewer's assumption directly.

questions.prompt: What does the translator mean by saying we meet most people 'only in their unrevised state'?
options: Most people refuse to change their opinions
We usually encounter people as their spontaneous, unedited selves, whereas she knew Almeida through his most carefully made self
Most people do not write letters
People are more honest in person than in writing
questions.explanation: 'We meet most people only in their unrevised state. I had the rare privilege of knowing someone almost entirely through the self he'd most carefully made.'
questions.hint: The translator contrasts 'unrevised' with the 'carefully made' self.

questions.prompt: Why, according to the translator, was there 'confessional freedom' in their correspondence?
options: Because writing to someone you'll never have to face allows you to confess things you couldn't say in person
Because the translator promised to keep secrets
questions.explanation: 'There's a confessional freedom in writing to someone you'll never have to face across a room.'
questions.hint: The translator links the freedom to never having to face him.

questions.prompt: Almeida wrote that the translator read him correctly only because she would always be 'slightly imaginary' to him. What does this suggest?
options: That the accurate reading depended on the relationship remaining partly imagined rather than fully real
That he wanted to keep her at a professional distance only
questions.explanation: 'this was only possible because I would always be slightly imaginary to him. He needed me imaginary.'
questions.hint: Consider why being 'imaginary' enabled the correct reading.

questions.prompt: How does the translator respond to the charge that they were each 'in love with a projection'?
options: She half agrees, but argues that most relationships are partly projections — theirs simply never faced the daily collision with reality
She says love was never involved
She agrees it was a mistake
questions.explanation: 'I'd half agree. We were partly in love with projections. But show me a relationship that isn't ... The difference is only that ours never had to survive the daily collision with reality.'
questions.hint: The translator concedes partly, then reframes the point.

questions.prompt: What is the translator NOT claiming about their relationship?
options: That it was a way of knowing a person
That something true passed between them
That it was definitively the better way to know a person
That a meeting would have 'spent' what they had
questions.explanation: 'I'm not claiming it's the better way to know a person. I'm claiming it's a way.'
questions.hint: She is careful to limit her claim in the 'projection' exchange.

questions.prompt: How does the translator answer whether she regrets never meeting him?
options: She does not regret it at all
She says she regrets it 'every day, and not at all', holding both truths without resolving them
questions.explanation: 'Every day, and not at all. Both are true and I've stopped trying to resolve them.'
questions.hint: The translator's final answer embraces a contradiction.

questions.prompt: What does the translator mean by saying Almeida 'stayed, for me, exactly the size of his sentences'?
options: He existed for her entirely within his writing, never enlarged or distorted by a physical presence
She forgot everything except his books
He shrank in importance after his death
questions.explanation: 'the clarity is undisturbed by any memory of a face. He stayed, for me, exactly the size of his sentences.'
questions.hint: Connect this to her wanting to avoid memories 'overwriting' the man.

questions.prompt: Why does the translator say she 'never had to lose a friend'?
options: Because she had been wise or cowardly enough never to fully acquire one — she lost only a correspondent
Because she has many other friends
Because friends cannot truly be lost
questions.explanation: 'I never had to lose a friend, because I'd been wise enough, or coward enough, never to fully acquire one.'
questions.hint: The final speech distinguishes a correspondent from a friend.

questions.prompt: Which of these two paraphrases is closer to the translator's overall stance?
options: Knowing someone only through their writing is a sad failure that should be avoided
Knowing someone through their writing is a distinct, possibly purer kind of intimacy whose value she cannot finally judge
Real relationships require physical meeting and theirs was therefore not real
Translation is more important than human connection
questions.explanation: She refuses to call it better or worse ('Whether that makes it less real or more concentrated ... I genuinely don't know') while insisting 'something true passed between us' — a deliberately unresolved, exploratory stance.
questions.hint: Notice her repeated refusal to settle the question definitively.

questions.prompt: The word 'rationalising' (translator's second speech) most nearly means:
options: inventing a reasonable-sounding justification for something done for other reasons
questions.explanation: 'You may be right that I'm rationalising' — offering a plausible reason that may disguise the real (perhaps avoidant) motive.
questions.hint: The interviewer suggested she was justifying 'avoidance'.

questions.prompt: Why does the translator end by inviting the interviewer to decide whether 'wise' or 'coward' is the fairer word?
options: Because she wants the interviewer's approval
Because she genuinely cannot resolve whether her choice was prudent self-protection or fearful avoidance
Because the words mean the same thing
questions.explanation: 'I'll let you decide which word is fairer. I've never managed to.' — she leaves her own motive honestly undecided.
questions.hint: Link this to her earlier 'I've stopped trying to resolve them'.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the tone the translator maintains throughout the interview?
options: Defensive and certain that her choice was right
Self-questioning and willing to hold contradictions without resolving them
Regretful and wishing she had acted differently
questions.explanation: She repeatedly concedes ('You may be right', 'I'd half agree', 'Both are true') while defending a nuanced position, never claiming final certainty.
questions.hint: Track her concessions and her refusals to resolve the paradoxes.

cloze.template:
Interviewer: You translated four of Almeida's novels, exchanged letters with him for over a decade, and yet you never once met in person. He lived in Porto; you live in Edinburgh. That's a ninety-minute flight. People find that almost {{b1}}. Why not meet?
Translator: I understand why it seems perverse. But it wasn't an accident, and it wasn't shyness. We discussed it, more than once, and we agreed. I think we both sensed that meeting would damage something we'd built precisely on the {{b2}} of not meeting. There's a kind of {{b3}} that only survives at a certain distance. Light a candle and bring it too close to glass and the glass cracks.
Interviewer: That sounds like a justification for avoidance, though. Forgive me, but couldn't you have had both — the letters and the friendship?
Translator: You may be right that I'm {{b4}}. I've wondered. But consider what the work actually required. To translate a man, I had to {{b5}} his sentences from the inside — his rhythms, his evasions, the particular way he'd circle a grief for three pages before naming it. I knew his prose more intimately than his wife did. What I did not know, and chose not to know, was the sound of his voice, his table manners, whether he interrupted. Those things would have arrived and {{b6}} the man I'd assembled from his syntax.
Interviewer: But surely the real man is more authentic than the one you assembled from his sentences?
Translator: Now that's the assumption I'd {{b7}} on. Why is the man at the dinner table the real one and the man in the prose a construction? He {{b8}} over those sentences for years. They were not less him than his table manners — arguably more, because they were the version of himself he chose and revised. We meet most people only in their {{b9}} state.
Interviewer: Did the letters ever stray into the personal?
Translator: Oh, deeply personal — but personal in writing, which is a different country. He told me things, I suspect, that he told no one he could see. There's a {{b10}} freedom in writing to someone you'll never have to face across a room. He once wrote that I was the only person who had ever read him correctly, and that this was only possible because I would always be slightly {{b11}} to him.
Interviewer: Some would say that's not a real relationship — that you were each in love with a projection.
Translator: I'd half agree. We were partly in love with projections. But show me a relationship that isn't, at least partly, the same thing. The difference is only that ours never had to survive the daily {{b12}} with reality that erodes the projection in an ordinary friendship. I'm not claiming it's the better way to know a person. I'm claiming it's a way, and that something true passed between us that a meeting would have {{b13}}.
Interviewer: He died last spring. Do you regret it now?
Translator: Every day, and not at all. Both are true and I've stopped trying to {{b14}} them. When I translate his last sentences, I hear him with total clarity, and the clarity is undisturbed by any memory of a face. I lost a correspondent. I never had to lose a friend, because I'd been wise enough, or {{b15}} enough, never to fully acquire one.

blanks.explanation: 'People find that almost perverse' — 'perverse' (deliberately contrary to what is reasonable) is the loaded word the translator then quotes back; 'strange/odd' are too mild.
'built precisely on the condition of not meeting' — 'condition' (a thing on which something else depends) is the exact fit; the relationship's existence was conditional on not meeting.
'a kind of intimacy that only survives at a certain distance' — 'intimacy' carries the paradox (deep closeness that needs distance) more precisely than 'friendship'.
'You may be right that I'm rationalising' — supplying a plausible reason for an act done on other grounds; the interviewer accused her of justifying 'avoidance'.
'I had to inhabit his sentences from the inside' — 'inhabit' (to live inside) conveys total immersion better than 'enter' or 'study'.
'would have ... overwritten the man I'd assembled' — 'overwritten' (written over, as data) precisely matches 'assembled from his syntax'.
'that's the assumption I'd push back on' — 'push back on' (resist, challenge) is the idiom for politely contesting a claim.
'He laboured over those sentences for years' — 'laboured over' (worked painstakingly at) is the fixed collocation conveying craft and effort.
'only in their unrevised state' — 'unrevised' (not edited or reworked) is the exact antonym of the 'carefully made' self and ties to the writing metaphor.
'a confessional freedom in writing' — 'confessional' (relating to disclosing secrets) precisely fits 'told me things ... he told no one he could see'.
'I would always be slightly imaginary to him' — 'imaginary' (existing only in imagination) is the word Almeida used; 'He needed me imaginary' follows.
'the daily collision with reality that erodes the projection' — 'collision' (a forceful, damaging clash) conveys the wearing-down the verb 'erodes' requires.
'something true ... that a meeting would have spent' — 'spent' (used up, exhausted, like money) is the surprising, precise choice the translator favours.
'I've stopped trying to resolve them' — 'resolve' (to settle a contradiction) fits holding two opposed truths; echoed by her closing 'I've never managed to'.
'wise enough, or coward enough' — the noun 'coward' (paired oddly with 'enough') creates the deliberate, unresolved self-judgement she leaves to the interviewer.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The translator argues that the version of a person they 'choose and revise' — their writing, their carefully made self — may be no less real than their unedited, in-person self. Think of someone you know mainly through their messages, posts, or work rather than face to face. Which version do you trust as 'the real them', and is the translator right that intimacy can sometimes deepen, rather than weaken, at a distance?
